sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A nasogastric (NG) tube is a flexible tube passed through the nose, down the oesophagus, into the stomach.

**Indications at Mine Site**
- **Medication administration:** activated charcoal in poisoning/overdose when patient cannot swallow voluntarily
- **Gastric decompression:** bowel obstruction, ileus, abdominal distension
- **Enteral nutrition/hydration:** in unconscious or obtunded patients during prolonged wait for RFDS (rare)

**Contraindications**
- **Suspected base of skull fracture** - insert orally (orogastric tube) instead; nasally-inserted tube may enter cranium through cribriform plate
- Bleeding diathesis (relative)
- Oesophageal obstruction, stricture, or varices (relative - discuss with RFDS)
- Uncontrolled coagulopathy (relative)
- Facial fractures involving nasal bones (insert orally)

sections.title: Equipment Required
sections.content:
**Equipment**
- 14–16Fr NG tube (Ryles tube) for adults; 8–10Fr for children
- Water-soluble lubricant
- 60 mL Toomey (bladder) syringe
- pH indicator strips (litmus/pH paper) - must confirm gastric placement
- Cup of water with straw (for cooperative patients)
- Adhesive tape for securing
- Gloves, apron, eye protection
- Kidney dish (patient may gag)

**Measuring Tube Length**
Measure externally before insertion:
- Nose → earlobe → xiphisternum
- Mark this distance on the tube with tape
- Adult: approximately 55–65 cm

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Preparation**
1. Explain procedure; position patient upright (high Fowler's 45–90 degrees) if conscious and able
2. Check nares - select the clearer nostril
3. Apply lubricant generously to the first 15 cm of tube
4. Have the patient hold a cup of water if conscious and safe to swallow

**Insertion**
1. **Advance tube** through chosen nostril, directing tip **posteriorly and inferiorly** (not straight back - angle toward ear, not toward brain)
2. Advance smoothly to the nasopharynx (approximately 10–15 cm) - resistance is normal here
3. **Ask patient to swallow** as you advance - swallowing opens the cricopharyngeus and directs tube into oesophagus not trachea
4. Continue advancing during each swallow, 2–3 cm per swallow
5. **If patient coughs violently, becomes cyanotic, or is unable to speak:** withdraw tube immediately - tube is likely in airway
6. Advance to the pre-marked distance
7. Temporarily secure with tape

**Confirming Placement - CRITICAL**
**pH method (primary):**
- Aspirate 5–10 mL of gastric contents using the Toomey syringe
- Apply to pH strip - gastric content is **pH ≤5.5**
- pH 5.5 or below = gastric placement confirmed - safe to use

**Do NOT use air insufflation/auscultation ("whoosh test")** - this method is unreliable and has led to fatalities from pulmonary installation of feed/medications

**If unable to aspirate:**
- Advance tube 5 cm and re-attempt aspiration
- Reposition patient (right lateral may assist aspiration)
- Ask patient to cough (dislodges tube from airway if misplaced)
- If still unable to confirm: do NOT use the tube; seek RFDS guidance

**Securing the Tube**
- Secure to nose with adhesive tape
- Loop tube behind ear for additional security
- Document external length at nares

sections.title: Troubleshooting and Complications
sections.content:
**Tube in Airway**
**Signs:**
- Patient coughs, chokes, cannot speak
- SpO2 drops
- Tube coils in mouth
- No gastric aspirate; unable to aspirate anything; resistance when aspirating

**Action:** Withdraw immediately and reattempt after patient recovers

**Coiling in Mouth or Pharynx**
- Tube is too soft or patient is not swallowing
- Withdraw to nasal passage, chill tube in ice water to stiffen, reattempt with more active swallowing

**Epistaxis**
- Apply pressure to nose; head forward
- Usually minor and self-limiting
- Use other nostril if bleeding significant

**Misplacement into Airway - Consequences**
- Administering activated charcoal, medication, or feed into the lung is rapidly fatal
- **Never use the tube without pH confirmation** regardless of urgency

**Intubated/Unconscious Patients**
- NG tube insertion in intubated patients: cuff must be inflated before insertion
- Laryngoscopy may assist if tube is difficult to pass
- Confirm with pH aspiration - unable to use swallowing technique

sections.title: Documentation and Maintenance
sections.content:
**Documentation**
- Time of insertion
- Nostril used and tube size
- Confirmation method and result (pH value, appearance of aspirate)
- External tube length at nares (to detect movement)
- Patient tolerance

**Tube Care**
- Re-confirm position before each use if tube has been in place or if there is any doubt
- Tubes can migrate - always re-check pH if there is any concern
- Flush with 30 mL water after each medication administration
- Secure tape may need replacing - inspect daily

**Removal**
- Pinch tube, remove in one smooth motion while patient holds breath
- Have suction available
